metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the execution-review `effective_block_threshold` formula to reflect that `threshold_for` returns `(float, bool)` — e.g. `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = threshold_for(criterion); effective_block_threshold = max(block_threshold, 0.80)` — not `max(threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)`.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/criteria/model.py:45-84: `def threshold_for(...) -> tuple[float, bool]` — returns a 2-tuple
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:598: `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = threshold_for(f.get('criteria', []))` — destructured as (float, bool)
Plan text: 'effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)' — treats return as scalar
findings.finding: The plan's execution-review Pass-3 formula `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` treats `threshold_for`'s return value as a float scalar, but the actual `threshold_for` implementation (criteria/model.py:45, review_kernel/decide.py:598) returns a 2-tuple `(float, bool)`. Calling `max(tuple, 0.80)` is a Python TypeError at runtime. The plan's interface assertion about `threshold_for`'s return type is factually wrong.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'For an execution review, normal Pass-3 resolution uses effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)'
scenarios: Implementing the formula literally produces a TypeError when comparing a (float, bool) tuple to 0.80.
An implementor must instead write max(threshold_for(criterion)[0], 0.80) — but the plan's assertion implies the wrong interface.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` with: `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = registry.threshold_for(criterion); effective_block_threshold = max(block_threshold, 0.80)` (and pass the unchanged `blocking_enabled` to `pass3_decide`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Pass-3 threshold formula to explicitly extract the float component: e.g., `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = registry.threshold_for(criterion); effective_block_threshold = max(block_threshold, 0.80)`.
evidence: Plan text: 'effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) for blocking criteria'
src/rebar/llm/criteria/model.py:45: `def threshold_for(...) -> tuple[float, bool]`
src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:309: `def threshold_for(...) -> tuple[float, bool]`
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:598: `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = threshold_for(f.get('criteria', []))`
`max(tuple[float, bool], 0.80)` is a Python TypeError; the plan never disambiguates which element is floored
findings.finding: The plan's Pass-3 threshold formula `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` is structurally type-incorrect: `threshold_for` returns `tuple[float, bool]` (confirmed in `src/rebar/llm/criteria/model.py:45` and `src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:309`), not a scalar float, so `max(tuple, 0.80)` is a Python type error. The plan never resolves which element of the tuple the `max` applies to (the `block_threshold` float, i.e., element [0]).
findings.location: Implementation § 'For an execution review, normal Pass-3 resolution uses effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)'
scenarios: An executor implementing the formula verbatim gets a TypeError at runtime.
Even a 'fix-by-inference' executor might apply the floor to `blocking_enabled` (the bool) rather than `block_threshold`, producing silent logic errors in blocking decisions.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the formula with: `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = _threshold_for(f.get('criteria', [])); effective_block_threshold = max(block_threshold, 0.80)` and pass `effective_block_threshold` into `pass3_decide(block_threshold=effective_block_threshold, blocking_enabled=blocking_enabled)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace vague 'run focused tests' with named test targets or pytest -k expressions that map each AC group (lifecycle, manifest parsing, threshold, sign-time concurrency, snapshot compaction) to a concrete command.
evidence: 'Run focused lifecycle, manifest, threshold, and sign-time concurrency tests' — no file names, no pytest -k expressions, no make targets
No AC-to-test mapping is provided for retry behavior, snapshot roll-forward, or lock-timeout ACs
'Run make check and the non-integration test suite' does not exercise the sign-time concurrency or snapshot compaction ACs
findings.finding: The Verification section names no concrete test commands — 'Run focused lifecycle, manifest, threshold, and sign-time concurrency tests' is a hedge that names test categories but not files, targets, or invocations. Many ACs (e.g., 'Three unstable-generation retries return a retryable unsigned result', 'An older reducer re-compacts a phase-bearing snapshot without stripping plan_review_phase') have no proving command mapping to them at all.
findings.location: Verification section: 'Run focused lifecycle, manifest, threshold, and sign-time concurrency tests.'
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm that the retry-count AC or snapshot roll-forward AC is actually covered by any test; a passing 'make check' gives no evidence for those specific claims.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Implementation section the concrete injection mechanism for the execution-phase threshold elevation: name whether a phase-aware wrapper replaces the `_threshold_for` closure in `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings`, or whether the orchestrator branches before calling `pass3_over_findings`.
evidence: Plan: 'For an execution review, normal Pass-3 resolution uses effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) for blocking criteria'
Codebase: orchestrator.py _threshold_for closure at line 694 is passed directly to review_kernel.pass3_over_findings; there is no existing hook for phase-conditional threshold elevation.
pass3_over_findings signature (decide.py line 579) accepts threshold_for as a plain callable with no phase parameter.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)` is applied in Pass-3 for execution reviews, but `pass3_over_findings` / `pass3_decide` accept a `threshold_for` callable that returns `(block_threshold, blocking_enabled)` as a pair. The plan does not specify how the execution-phase elevated threshold is injected into the existing `threshold_for` closure in `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` — whether by wrapping the existing resolver, by branching in the orchestrator before calling `pass3_over_findings`, or by a new parameter. Without this, the implementer must invent an injection point that may silently fail to apply the elevated threshold.
findings.location: Implementation section, fifth bullet; AC 'Execution review Pass-3 decisions apply max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) only to blocking criteria'
scenarios: Implementer wraps the threshold_for closure but applies it unconditionally (not just for execution phase), silently hardening planning reviews.
Implementer applies the elevation after pass3_over_findings returns, re-adjudicating decisions, which breaks the deterministic kernel contract.
Implementer adds a phase parameter to pass3_over_findings, creating a kernel API change not covered by the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact manifest line format for both new fields (e.g., `review-phase: <value>` and `priority-floor: <value>`) including the prefix string, separator, and value grammar, co-located with the `build_manifest` ordering description.
evidence: Plan names parser functions `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` but gives no prefix string (compare: existing manifest.py uses `_REGVER_PREFIX = 'regver:'`, `_DEP_PREFIX = 'dep'`, etc.)
The existing `build_manifest` in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py has no `review_phase` or `priority_floor` parameter — an implementer must invent the line format
No line grammar is specified: e.g., is it `review-phase: planning` or `review-phase:planning`? Is `priority-floor:` a colon-separated or space-separated format?
ABSENCE: no `_REVIEW_PHASE_PREFIX` or `_PRIORITY_FLOOR_PREFIX` constant is described anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The manifest line format for `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` is never given a concrete token grammar. The plan names `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` as parsers and describes their error cases, but never specifies the line prefix strings, token counts, or separator characters — the same information `build_manifest` needs to emit them and the parsers need to consume them.
findings.location: Implementation § 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version. manifest_review_phase() returns legacy planning only when absent; manifest_priority_floor() returns None only when absent.'
scenarios: The emitter in `build_manifest` and the parsers `manifest_review_phase()`/`manifest_priority_floor()` may produce non-matching line formats, causing parse failures on every attestation.
The duplicate-detection and ManifestFormatError logic cannot be implemented without knowing the prefix string to look for.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a manifest-format table analogous to the existing `dep <sha256> <path>` and `regver: <stamp>` conventions, e.g.: `review-phase: planning|execution` and `priority-floor: <float 0.0–1.0>`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Verification section with the integration-test AC: either add integration tests to the verification run or remove/replace the AC requiring them.
evidence: Verification: 'Run make check and the non-integration test suite.'
AC: 'A production-path integration test exercises stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, and signature append plus commit end to end.'
findings.finding: The Verification section says 'Run make check and the non-integration test suite,' explicitly excluding the integration suite, but AC requires 'A production-path integration test exercises stable collection, under-lock HEAD re-verification, and signature append plus commit end to end.' These two sections directly contradict each other on whether integration tests are run.
findings.location: Verification section vs AC line: 'A production-path integration test exercises stable collection...'
scenarios: The integration test required by the AC is never run as part of plan verification, leaving the sign-time concurrency path untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Verification section to: 'Run focused lifecycle, manifest, threshold, and sign-time concurrency tests, including the production-path integration test for stable collection and signature append. Run make check and the full test suite.'

evidence: 31 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add implementation guidance describing how each of the six named consumers (claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, health) enforces phase incompatibility.
evidence: ABSENCE: Implementation section only describes pre-sign transaction behavior; claim/close profile/reuse/re-sign/audit/health are not mentioned.
AC text names six distinct consumers.
findings.finding: The AC requires phase incompatibility to be enforced by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health, but the Implementation section describes only the pre-sign transaction path. None of the other six consumers (claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, health) have any implementation guidance.
findings.location: AC: 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health.'
scenarios: Implementer enforces phase incompatibility only in the sign path and leaves the other five consumers unguarded, satisfying tests but not the actual protocol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling path for LLM call failures in the pre-sign transaction: classify as retryable or terminal unsigned, define rollback/cleanup of any pre-LLM committed snapshots, and cap LLM call attempts.
evidence: 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM. Afterward, on the clean tickets tracker…'
No mention of LLM timeout, LLM error codes, or what happens to pre-LLM snapshots if the LLM call never completes.
AC items 29–31 cover HEAD mismatch and LockTimeout retries but are silent on LLM-call failure modes.
findings.finding: The pre-sign transaction section describes what happens before and after the LLM call, and covers HEAD-mismatch and LockTimeout retries, but does not specify how an LLM call failure (timeout, network error, non-retryable error) is handled — whether it is surfaced as a retryable or terminal unsigned result, and whether any partial state written before the call is rolled back.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Own the unified pre-sign transaction' paragraph
scenarios: LLM provider is down or times out mid-review; pre-LLM snapshots are committed but signing never completes, leaving an inconsistent partial pre-sign state.
Transient LLM error retried indefinitely blocks the write lock.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'If the LLM call fails or times out, discard pre-LLM snapshots (no commit), emit a structured ERROR log, and return retryable_unsigned up to N attempts, then terminal_unsigned.' Align with the existing LockTimeout/HEAD-mismatch retry AC items.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `build_manifest` ordering change for `review-phase` and `priority-floor` to account for the fact that `rebar-version` is currently in the fixed initial `lines` list (index 7), not a trailing optional line — explicitly specify whether the fixed block must be split or whether the new lines go before the optional-line section (after rebar-version).
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py:190-200: `lines = [f'{_MANIFEST_PREFIX}: ...', 'ticket: ...', 'material: ...', 'model: ...', 'runner: ...', 'blocking: ...', 'advisory: ...', _signing.rebar_version_step(...)]` — rebar-version is index 7 of the fixed literal list, not a trailing optional line
All optional lines (regver, disabled_builtins, refreshed_from, verified_at_sha, dep) are appended AFTER this fixed block via conditional `lines.append(...)` calls
The plan's ordering places new lines 'before rebar-version', but rebar-version is inside the fixed literal, not at the boundary between fixed and optional sections
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the new `review-phase` and `priority-floor` manifest lines are inserted 'immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version' in `build_manifest`'s final order. However, the actual `build_manifest` implementation (manifest.py:190-200) places `rebar-version` as the 8th entry of the FIXED initial `lines` list, constructed before any optional lines. The advisory line (index 6) and rebar-version line (index 7) are both in the same fixed block with no insertion point between them without restructuring the fixed block itself. The plan's ordering claim is unverifiable as stated against the actual code structure.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version.'
scenarios: An implementor following the plan's ordering literally must restructure the fixed `lines` block — splitting it at the advisory/rebar-version boundary — which is a larger refactor than the plan implies.
If instead the new lines are simply appended after the fixed block (the natural insertion point), they appear AFTER rebar-version, contradicting the stated ordering.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or design-doc update capturing at minimum: the decision to store plan_review_phase in compiled state (vs. derivation on demand), the fixed 0.80 floor protocol policy and its intentional non-configurability, the manifest format extension contract (ordering, malformed rules), and the pre-sign transaction locking/retry protocol.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan

Novel decisions include: reducer-maintained enum (paragraph 1), manifest format extension (paragraph 3), fixed 0.80 protocol policy with evolution rationale (paragraph 4), max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) override rule (paragraph 5), unified pre-sign transaction protocol (paragraph 6)
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions (reducer-maintained plan_review_phase enum in compiled state, new manifest format lines for review-phase and priority-floor, a new phase-aware unified pre-sign transaction, and execution vs. planning review modes with distinct threshold overrides) but mentions no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update to capture these decisions and their rationale for future maintainers.
findings.location: Implementation section (all paragraphs) and Verification section
scenarios: A future engineer changing the manifest parser encounters the priority-floor-without-review-phase malformed rule with no authoritative design document explaining why it was designed this way
The 0.80 floor policy is revisited and the reasoning behind making it non-configurable is undiscoverable without the original author
A new component integrator reads the pre-sign transaction protocol and has no canonical reference for the locking/retry contract
findings.suggested_fix: Create a short ADR (or append to an existing AGENTS.md/design doc) with sections for each of the four novel architectural decisions; reference it from the Implementation section of this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement for the bootstrap path: emit a structured INFO or DEBUG log (including plan id, compiled status, and assigned phase) whenever plan_review_phase is bootstrapped on first post-upgrade reduction.
evidence: 'bootstrap planning for compiled status open or idea and execution otherwise' — no log or metric accompanies this assignment
AC item 8 ('Reducer-cache versioning activates bootstrap idempotently') verifies idempotency but not observability
AC item 33 covers revision-retry and signing-abort logs, but nothing for bootstrap
findings.finding: No observability (log, metric, or trace) is specified for the bootstrap phase-assignment path executed on the first post-upgrade reduction of pre-feature snapshots. If the bootstrap applies the wrong phase (e.g., due to an unrecognized compiled status), there is no signal for operators or developers to detect it.
findings.location: Implementation — 'When a pre-feature snapshot lacks plan_review_phase, bootstrap…' paragraph; AC items 6–8
scenarios: A compiled status that doesn't cleanly map to open/idea/other silently falls into the 'execution' bucket; the mis-assigned phase blocks reviews with no diagnostic.
Post-upgrade reducer bug assigns planning to an in_progress plan; the wrong phase is silently persisted in all subsequent snapshots.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the pre-sign transaction atomicity mechanism (HEAD re-verification, retry loop, LockTimeout handling, structured logs, integration test AC) into a separate plan so each concern can be reviewed, implemented, and verified independently.
evidence: Goal states: 'Allow plan changes made during implementation to be reviewed without re-litigating low-priority implementation findings' — signing atomicity/concurrency is not mentioned.
Title is 'Add planning and execution phases to plan-review attestations' — no reference to pre-sign transaction safety.
Six of ~30 ACs (roughly 20%) and one full implementation paragraph are dedicated to the pre-sign transaction, which is an independently deployable concern.
findings.finding: The plan bundles a substantial new pre-sign concurrency mechanism (HEAD re-verification, outside-lock retry loop, LockTimeout handling, structured logs, integration test) into a plan whose title and goal are exclusively about adding planning/execution phase distinction to attestations. This is scope drift doing MORE than the title promises; the signing transaction concern is separable from phase semantics and could be independently planned, reviewed, and tested.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Own the unified pre-sign transaction' + Acceptance Criteria lines 'Unified pre-sign comparison aborts…', 'Stable generation permits…', 'Three unstable-generation retries…', 'Pre-sign LockTimeout…', 'A production-path integration test…', 'Revision retries emit structured WARN logs…'
scenarios: An implementer building only the phase-distinction feature may over-engineer or introduce bugs in the signing path that would have been caught by a focused signing-safety plan review.

A reviewer of this plan cannot clearly gate whether the pre-sign transaction changes are complete without knowing what the prior signing contract was, because no prior behavior is described for that mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the 'Own the unified pre-sign transaction' implementation paragraph and its six corresponding ACs into a separate plan titled something like 'Harden plan-review signing atomicity with HEAD verification and retry logic'; reference it as a dependency or follow-on from the current plan if ordering matters.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the pre-sign concurrency/retry/LockTimeout protocol (ACs 25–31) can be sequenced as a follow-on hardening increment after the phase-semantics core ships, or at minimum document why the coupling to phase-comparison makes separation impractical.
evidence: 'Own the unified pre-sign transaction. Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM.'
'Retry outside-lock collection at most three times on revision change, then return retryable unsigned. LockTimeout during the pre-sign commit also returns retryable unsigned.'

ACs 25–31 are self-contained (stable generation, retry exhaustion, LockTimeout, integration test, WARN/ERROR logging, compaction roll-forward) and do not reference the planning/execution phase semantics — they stand on their own correctness argument.

Distinct reason-to-change: the retry count, LockTimeout policy, and log schema can evolve for operational reasons (e.g. tuning retry count from 3 to 5) independently of any change to planning vs. execution phase semantics.
findings.finding: The pre-sign transaction section (unified pre-sign comparison, retry loop, LockTimeout handling, structured WARN/ERROR logging) introduces a distinct operational-safety/concurrency-hardening concern with its own reason to change — infrastructure correctness — separate from the plan_review_phase feature logic. This bundles two actors' concerns: the feature team adding phase semantics, and the infrastructure/ops concern of signing-protocol race-condition prevention. While the phase-comparison is part of the pre-sign check (creating coupling), the retry-count policy, LockTimeout classification, and structured-log schema are independently motivated and could shift for operational reasons unrelated to the phase feature.
findings.location: Implementation section — final paragraph ('Own the unified pre-sign transaction...'); ACs 25–31
scenarios: A future operator decides to change the retry limit from 3 to 5 for latency reasons — a pure operational change that has nothing to do with phase semantics forces a modification to the same unit.
A concurrency bug in the signing protocol is found post-ship; the fix requires reasoning about and re-testing all phase-comparison logic to avoid regression, inflating scope unnecessarily.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` (`_snapshot_strip_keys`) as an explicit edit target, confirming `plan_review_phase` must not appear in its strip set.
evidence: _snapshot_strip_keys() currently returns {'updated_at', 'signature'} (compact.py:142)
compact.py:262 applies this strip to compiled_state before writing the SNAPSHOT event
The plan says plan_review_phase must survive compaction but does not name compact.py as an edit target
If plan_review_phase is accidentally added to this set (e.g. by a future maintainer), the compaction invariant silently breaks
findings.finding: compact.py's _snapshot_strip_keys() function is an unenumerated edit target: it controls which keys are dropped from compiled_state during compaction, and the new plan_review_phase field must not appear in that set for the 'Carry it through SNAPSHOT compaction' requirement to hold.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Carry it through SNAPSHOT compaction'; src/rebar/_commands/compact.py:136
scenarios: Implementor forgets to verify that _snapshot_strip_keys does NOT include plan_review_phase, silently stripping the projection on every compact
AC 'Compaction snapshots preserve the projection' passes in unit test but fails in integration because compact.py was not updated
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Implementation: 'Verify that _snapshot_strip_keys() in _commands/compact.py does not include plan_review_phase (no edit needed if it is absent, but the explicit check guards against accidental addition).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py` to the Implementation as an explicit edit target, specifying how it reads the ticket's compiled plan_review_phase and passes it into build_manifest (or delegates to the new unified pre-sign transaction).
evidence: resign_plan_review() calls sign_plan_review() without any phase-compatibility check on the sidecar payload
The Implementation section describes the unified pre-sign transaction and build_manifest changes but never names resign.py
If resign_plan_review re-signs using the old build_manifest it will produce a phase-bearing attestation that might not match the ticket's compiled phase
findings.finding: resign_plan_review (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py) is listed in AC as a consumer that must enforce phase incompatibility but is not named in the Implementation section as an edit target.
findings.location: AC: 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py
scenarios: An execution-phase ticket's cheap re-sign path produces a manifest with review-phase:planning (or no phase), violating the phase-compatibility invariant the AC requires
The AC 're-sign' enforcement passes in sign_plan_review but not in resign_plan_review, leaving the cheap-re-sign path as a phase-bypass

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `refresh_attestation()` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` as an edit target, specifying that it must forward the prior manifest's review-phase and priority-floor lines (or re-read the ticket's compiled phase) into the new build_manifest call.
evidence: drift_refresh_candidate() calls manifest_regver(), manifest_material(), manifest_deps() on the existing manifest — all prefix-scan based, so insertion is safe

However, refresh_attestation() (attest.py:350) calls build_manifest() directly and reconstructs a manifest from a prior one; the new phase/floor fields would need to be forwarded from the prior manifest or re-derived from compiled state
refresh_attestation does not pass review-phase or priority-floor to build_manifest, so it would produce a phase-less manifest even for a post-upgrade execution-phase ticket
findings.finding: drift_refresh_candidate() and remediation_mode_candidate() in attest.py parse existing manifest lines (e.g. manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_deps) and may be broken or produce silent mismatches if the new review-phase and priority-floor lines are inserted into build_manifest's fixed order at a position that shifts parsing offsets.
findings.location: Implementation: 'In build_manifest final order, insert review-phase immediately after advisory, then optional priority-floor, before rebar-version'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:146-179, 182-298
scenarios: A drift-refreshed attestation for an execution-phase ticket loses its review-phase:execution line, causing the next claim_gate_check to read it as a planning attestation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the reducer-cache-version-triggered re-reduction is lazy (per-ticket on first access) or eager (background sweep), and for the eager case add batching/throttling controls or document the maximum safe corpus size for an atomic upgrade.
evidence: 'Bump the reducer-cache version so first post-upgrade reduction applies this idempotently' — implies all tickets are re-reduced on first access after upgrade, potentially simultaneously.
No mention of batching, throttling, staged rollout, or background migration job.
No upper bound stated on how many concurrent re-reductions the system supports.
findings.finding: The reducer-cache version bump forces re-reduction of every ticket on first post-upgrade access; for deployments with large numbers of tickets, this constitutes an unthrottled re-compaction wave. The plan provides no batching, throttling, or staged rollout mechanism for this burst of write activity.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Bump the reducer-cache version so first post-upgrade reduction applies this idempotently; subsequent snapshots persist it.'
scenarios: Large deployment with thousands of tickets: every ticket accessed in the first minutes post-upgrade triggers a full re-reduction and snapshot write, saturating storage I/O and potentially causing latency spikes or timeouts.
If re-reduction is triggered eagerly (e.g., by a background compaction job) rather than lazily, the entire corpus is re-reduced in one burst.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the live STATUS-event phase rule for a winning transition targeting 'idea' (e.g., 'targeting idea also sets planning, matching the bootstrap mapping').
evidence: Implementation says 'one targeting open sets planning' but does not mention 'idea'
Bootstrap rule says 'bootstrap planning for compiled status open or idea' — confirming 'idea' is a valid compiled status
No 'other winning targets preserve it' phrase covers this if idea is a targetable STATUS transition the same way open is
findings.finding: The implementation describes live STATUS event phase rules only for 'in_progress' (→ execution) and 'open' (→ planning), but 'idea' appears only in the bootstrap rule. An executor cannot determine whether a winning STATUS event targeting 'idea' should set planning, set execution, or preserve the current projection — the live-transition rule is silent on it.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 1: 'a winning committed STATUS event targeting in_progress sets execution, one targeting open sets planning'
scenarios: A ticket transitions from planning→idea (or similar) via a winning STATUS event; the executor must decide whether to write planning or leave the phase unchanged, with no guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the implementation sentence to: '…targeting in_progress sets execution, one targeting open or idea sets planning, and other winning targets preserve it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health' into six separate ACs, one per named subsystem, each with its own verifiable test.
evidence: Six named subsystems (claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, health) each represent an independently verifiable enforcement point
A single test or command cannot simultaneously prove all six paths enforce the rule; each needs its own fixture
The criterion is phrased as a consistency property across the system, which is verifiable only by testing each path individually
findings.finding: The AC 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health' bundles six distinct enforcement subsystems into a single criterion, making it an epic-of-epics that cannot be verified as a single observable outcome and is not a right-sized single-outcome deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health.'
scenarios: An implementer completes and tests 5 of 6 paths; the single AC is marked done, but one enforcement point is silently missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an implementation description for DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status covering its three values (compatible, incompatible, malformed) and how they map to the planning/execution comparison.
evidence: ABSENCE: The Implementation section never mentions DerivedPlanReviewHealth or phase_status.
AC text: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed under the documented planning/execution comparison.'
findings.finding: DerivedPlanReviewHealth and its phase_status field (values: compatible, incompatible, malformed) are named in the AC but have no corresponding implementation description anywhere in the plan body, making the AC an orphan with no described deliverable to map to.
findings.location: AC: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed under the documented planning/execution comparison.'
scenarios: An implementer has no implementation guidance for how to compute or represent phase_status on DerivedPlanReviewHealth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an implementation note describing the completion-verifier branch and explicitly stating why it must not invoke manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins().
evidence: ABSENCE: Implementation section does not mention completion-verifier.
The constraint on which manifest functions the completion-verifier must NOT call implies a described design decision that is absent.
findings.finding: The AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()' describes a constraint on the completion-verifier that has no corresponding description in the Implementation section — neither the completion-verifier's role nor its relationship to these manifest functions is mentioned.
findings.location: AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins().'
scenarios: An implementer does not know why the completion-verifier must avoid these functions, or even where the completion-verifier is, leading to accidental invocation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Implementation section whether 'related-fingerprint' is a field of PlanRelationSnapshot or a separate concept, and use consistent terminology in the AC.
evidence: AC text: 'related-fingerprint change'
Implementation text: 'PlanRelationSnapshot' — no mention of 'related-fingerprint' as a distinct concept.
findings.finding: The AC uses the term 'related-fingerprint' ('aborts after any phase, own-material, relationship, or related-fingerprint change') but the Implementation section uses 'PlanRelationSnapshot' without defining what 'related-fingerprint' is or confirming it is a field within PlanRelationSnapshot. This terminology mismatch makes the AC ambiguous.
findings.location: AC: 'Unified pre-sign comparison aborts after any phase, own-material, relationship, or related-fingerprint change.' vs Implementation: 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM.'
scenarios: Implementer is unsure whether 'related-fingerprint' is a field inside PlanRelationSnapshot, a separate concept, or an alias — leading to under- or over-checking at sign time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the reducer-state and manifest schema changes, describing how a revert to a pre-feature version handles snapshots and attestations that already carry plan_review_phase and priority-floor.
evidence: Implementation describes reducer-cache version bump and bootstrap as a one-way migration.
No rollback/back-out plan is described for reverting the schema change.
AC addresses only one direction of the older-reducer scenario.
findings.finding: The plan adds plan_review_phase to compiled reducer state (a schema change) and bumps the reducer-cache version, but provides no rollback path. If the feature is reverted, older reducers will encounter snapshots containing plan_review_phase with no described handling. The AC about 'An older reducer re-compacts a phase-bearing snapshot' addresses only forward compatibility (older code reading newer snapshots in one direction) but does not address rollback.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Add an internal reducer-maintained plan_review_phase enum to compiled state... Bump the reducer-cache version... Carry it through SNAPSHOT compaction.' AC: 'An older reducer re-compacts a phase-bearing snapshot without stripping plan_review_phase, and roll-forward restores the exact value.'
scenarios: A deployment is rolled back to a pre-feature version; older reducers encounter snapshots with plan_review_phase and either error or silently drop it, potentially corrupting phase enforcement state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomicity mechanism (e.g., CAS on snapshot version, write lock, or conditional put) that ensures concurrent bootstrap reductions produce exactly one phase-bearing snapshot and subsequent reductions skip bootstrap.
evidence: 'Bump the reducer-cache version so first post-upgrade reduction applies this idempotently; subsequent snapshots persist it' — mechanism for concurrent safety of the snapshot write is absent
The global write lock is described for the signing path but not for the compaction/bootstrap snapshot write
No CAS or check-then-write guard is described for the bootstrap snapshot
findings.finding: The plan claims bootstrap idempotency via reducer-cache versioning ('Bump the reducer-cache version so first post-upgrade reduction applies this idempotently'), but does not specify what prevents two concurrent processes from simultaneously reducing the same pre-feature snapshot and both executing the bootstrap — nor whether the resulting snapshot write is atomic, which is required for the idempotency guarantee to hold under concurrency.
findings.location: Implementation — 'When a pre-feature snapshot lacks plan_review_phase, bootstrap…' paragraph; AC item 8
scenarios: Two API workers simultaneously process the same plan on upgrade; both see a phase-less snapshot, both bootstrap, both attempt to write a new snapshot; one write wins silently, or both succeed with potential ordering anomalies depending on storage semantics.
A bootstrap write fails mid-way; the reducer-cache version is bumped but the snapshot is not persisted, causing repeated re-bootstrap on every subsequent reduction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the user-facing error message content and remediation path for phase-incompatible attestations (e.g., 'submit a new execution-phase review with priority-floor ≥ 0.80') at each enforcing surface (claim, close, re-sign, audit, health).
evidence: AC item 18: 'Phase incompatibility is enforced consistently by claim, close profile, reuse, re-sign, audit, and health' — enforcement is specified but not what the user sees or does next
AC item 17: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed' — the three states are named but error messaging and remediation path are absent
No AC item describes the user flow for obtaining a new execution-phase review after a plan moves from open to in_progress with an existing planning review
findings.finding: When phase incompatibility is detected at claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit, or health check surfaces, the plan specifies that incompatibility is enforced (AC item 18) and that DerivedPlanReviewHealth returns one of three states (AC item 17), but provides no specification of user-facing remediation guidance or the UX flow for upgrading a planning-phase review to execution-phase after a plan transitions to in_progress.
findings.location: AC items 17–18; Implementation — 'Planning mode accepts only planning. Execution mode accepts…' paragraph
scenarios: A reviewer submits a planning review, the plan transitions to in_progress, and a second reviewer tries to claim it — the claim is rejected as incompatible with no guidance on what to do next.
An operator running an audit sees 'incompatible' health status with no remediation steps documented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what `manifest_pins()` refers to in the completion-verifier AC: either add it to the Implementation section as a new manifest parser being created, or remove it from the AC if it is not a real symbol.
evidence: search_files for 'manifest_pins' returns zero matches across the entire src/rebar tree
The Implementation section creates manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() but never mentions manifest_pins()
No symbol by this name appears in manifest.py, attest.py, or any other file
findings.finding: manifest_pins() is referenced in an AC line but does not exist anywhere in the codebase and is not created by this ticket in the Implementation section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
scenarios: The AC cannot be verified against a non-existent function, leaving the invariant unenforceable in tests
If manifest_pins() is meant to be a new function introduced by this ticket, the Implementation is missing its definition
findings.suggested_fix: If manifest_pins() is intended to guard against a new 'pins:' line being accidentally read in the completion-verifier path, declare its creation in the manifest.py section of the Implementation alongside manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor().

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the Implementation section explaining why reducer-maintained compiled-state (rather than query-time status inference or signing-time annotation) is the correct approach for plan_review_phase.
evidence: The implementation paragraph opens 'Add an internal reducer-maintained plan_review_phase enum to compiled state' with no preceding 'because' or rationale clause.
The plan states 'Review reads it O(1), never scans raw history' as a property but not as a rationale for choosing compiled state.
No section of the plan explains why reducer-maintained state was chosen over query-time inference or signing-time annotation.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for the chosen reducer-maintained compiled-state approach to tracking plan_review_phase. The Implementation jumps directly to mechanism (which field, how it is set, how it is compacted) without explaining why this approach is superior to alternatives such as query-time inference from current status or a signing-time-only annotation on SIGNATURE events. The absence of a positive rationale makes it harder for implementers to recognize when a corner-case implementation decision is consistent with the design intent.
findings.location: Implementation section, first bullet
scenarios: An implementer uncertain whether to derive phase at read-time (cheaper to implement, no migration) has no documented rationale to resolve the question.
A future author proposing a rollback to query-time inference cannot point to a documented reason it was rejected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence such as: 'Compiled state is required because query-time status inference misclassifies tickets that are currently open after returning from in_progress (they should remain planning), and signing-time annotation would require O(signature-event) scanning rather than O(1) reads.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the completion-verifier isolation AC with the parent's explicit assignment: either remove it from this (phase) story and confirm it lives in the validity story (75de), or add a note clarifying that the phase story covers only the manifest_review_phase/manifest_priority_floor half and 75de covers manifest_pins().
evidence: Parent epic comment (eighth review): 'accepted f7ccea40cde985d77 with completion-verifier isolation owned and tested by the validity child'
Parent epic settled design: 'the completion-verifier branch does not parse plan-review-only phase, floor, or pins'
Leaf AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
manifest_pins() is defined in the pins story (acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c), not the phase story (5967)
findings.finding: The leaf claims the AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()' but the parent's epic (eighth review disposition comment) explicitly attributes 'completion-verifier isolation owned and tested by the validity child' to ticket 75de-02b0-d454-410b, not to this phase story. The leaf is asserting ownership of a verification responsibility the parent delegated to a sibling, which puts both stories in scope conflict over where that test lives.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
scenarios: Two stories (phase/5967 and validity/75de) each believe they own the completion-verifier isolation test; one is written and the other is omitted, leaving a gap.
The phase story implements a test that calls manifest_pins() before the pins story (acaf) lands, causing a broken import or test dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the manifest_pins() reference from this AC (keeping manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() since the phase story does own those parsers), and confirm that the full three-parser isolation test lives in 75de as the parent directed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and assert the post-rollback schema state: specify whether a pre-feature reducer ignores unknown snapshot fields gracefully (unknown-field tolerance must be verified), and add a back-out AC confirming that rolling back to pre-feature code leaves all compacted snapshots readable and correctly projected (or document a compensating-forward procedure).
evidence: 'When a pre-feature snapshot lacks plan_review_phase, bootstrap planning for compiled status open or idea and execution otherwise.'
AC tests an 'older reducer' within the new system but does not define behavior of a full pre-feature rollback encountering new-format snapshots.
POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE is not asserted: the plan does not state that reverting the deployment leaves snapshots in a valid, readable state.
DATA LOSS RISK if pre-feature code errors on unexpected snapshot fields and event-log retention is insufficient for full re-reduction.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the post-rollback schema state when pre-feature code encounters snapshots that already carry `plan_review_phase`. AC 'An older reducer re-compacts a phase-bearing snapshot without stripping plan_review_phase' covers within-generation forward compat but does not address full pre-feature code rollback. If the old reducer errors or silently drops the field, compacted snapshots become unreadable or lose the projection, and re-reduction from raw events may not be possible if event-log retention is limited.
findings.location: Implementation section (bootstrap paragraph) and AC: 'An older reducer re-compacts a phase-bearing snapshot without stripping plan_review_phase, and roll-forward restores the exact value.'
scenarios: Operator rolls back to pre-feature binary after some snapshots have been compacted with plan_review_phase; old reducer fails to parse snapshot and ticket state becomes inaccessible.
Event-log retention window has expired for old events; re-reduction from log is impossible; rolled-back system cannot reconstruct projection.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC: 'A pre-feature (phase-unaware) reducer encountering a phase-bearing snapshot either (a) ignores unknown fields and re-reduces correctly from the event log, or (b) the rollback procedure strips plan_review_phase from snapshots via a compensating migration before old code is restarted.' Also state the minimum event-log retention window required for full re-reduction as a deployment prerequisite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` as a schema in the Implementation section, listing its fields (including `phase_status`), its three-value enum vocabulary (`compatible`, `incompatible`, `malformed`), and the decision table mapping `(manifest_review_phase, ticket_plan_review_phase)` pairs to each value.
evidence: AC text: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed'
search_files for 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth' in src/ returns zero matches — the type does not exist
The plan body never defines the DerivedPlanReviewHealth struct, its fields, or the decision rule mapping (review_phase, ticket_phase) → phase_status value
The three enum values appear only in the AC line, not in Implementation
findings.finding: `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.phase_status` is referenced in AC 17 with a three-value vocabulary (`compatible`, `incompatible`, `malformed`) but neither `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` nor its `phase_status` field schema is defined anywhere in this plan. The plan names the values only in the AC, not co-located with any schema or processing protocol section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth phase_status is exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed under the documented planning/execution comparison'
scenarios: An executor has no schema definition to implement — must infer field names, types, and the compatibility matrix from AC prose alone.
A second implementer (e.g., the health-check consumer in claim/close/reuse) has no shared contract to reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `PlanRelationSnapshot`'s field schema in this plan's Implementation section (or explicitly reference the sibling plan that owns it), and specify the exact equality predicate used in the pre-sign before/after comparison.
evidence: search_files for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' in src/ returns zero matches — the type does not exist
The plan body states 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM' but never defines the PlanRelationSnapshot schema in this plan's Implementation section
AC 'compare exact snapshots' requires field-level equality — impossible to implement without knowing what fields PlanRelationSnapshot contains
The comparison rule ('aborts after any phase, own-material, relationship, or related-fingerprint change') lists four dimensions but never maps them to PlanRelationSnapshot fields
findings.finding: `PlanRelationSnapshot` is referenced in the pre-sign transaction section as a named type whose snapshot is taken before the LLM call and compared afterward, but its field schema (fields, types, what constitutes a 'change') is never defined within this plan. The plan treats it as a known type but it does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM. Afterward, on the clean tickets tracker...' and AC: 'Unified pre-sign comparison aborts after any phase, own-material, relationship, or related-fingerprint change.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the pre-sign transaction cannot write the before/after comparison without knowing the PlanRelationSnapshot fields.
The retry loop's termination condition ('retry unless equal') is undefined without a concrete equality operator on PlanRelationSnapshot.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
